problem: For any t∈ (0,1) let t=∑_{k=1}^∞ \epsilon_k(t)2^{-k} (where \epsilon_k(t)∈ \{0,1\}). What is the correct order of magnitude (for almost all t∈(0,1)) forM_n(t)=\max_{x∈ [-1,1]}\left| ∑_{k≤ n}(-1)^{\epsilon_k(t)}x^k\right|?
original_latex: For any $t\in (0,1)$ let $t=\sum_{k=1}^\infty \epsilon_k(t)2^{-k}$ (where $\epsilon_k(t)\in \{0,1\}$). What is the correct order of magnitude (for almost all $t\in(0,1)$) for\[M_n(t)=\max_{x\in [-1,1]}\left\lvert \sum_{k\leq n}(-1)^{\epsilon_k(t)}x^k\right\rvert?\]
additional_context:
A problem of Salem and Zygmund \cite{SaZy54}. Chung showed that, for almost all t, there exist infinitely many n such thatM_n(t) \ll \left((n)/(\log\log n)\right)^{1/2}.Erdős (unpublished) showed that for almost all t and every \epsilon>0 we have \lim_{n→ ∞}M_n(t)/n^{1/2-\epsilon}=∞.

References

[SaZy54] Salem, R. and Zygmund, A., Some properties of trigonometric series whose terms have
random signs. Acta Math. (1954), 245-301.
reference_proof_hint:
Let
[
a_k(t):=(-1)^{\epsilon_k(t)}\in{\pm1},\qquad P_n(x):=\sum_{k=1}^n a_k(t),x^k,
\qquad M_n(t)=\sup_{x\in[-1,1]}|P_n(x)|.
]
For Lebesgue-a.e. (t\in(0,1)), the bits (\epsilon_k(t)) are i.i.d. Bernoulli($1/2$), hence ((a_k(t))) are i.i.d. Rademacher [[nomath]]($\pm1$ with prob. $1/2$)[[/nomath]]. (We ignore the dyadic rationals, a null set, where the binary expansion is not unique.)

## Step 1: Reduce the supremum over $x$ to random-walk partial sums

Define the partial sums
[
A_m:=\sum_{k=1}^m a_k(t),\qquad m\le n.
]

**Abel/partial summation** gives, for (x\in[0,1]),
[
\sum_{k=1}^n a_k x^k
= A_n x^n+\sum_{m=1}^{n-1} A_m,(x^m-x^{m+1}).
]
Since (x^m-x^{m+1}=x^m(1-x)\ge 0) on $[0,1]$,
[
\Big|\sum_{k=1}^n a_k x^k\Big|
\le \Big(\max_{1\le m\le n}|A_m|\Big)\Big(x^n+\sum_{m=1}^{n-1}(x^m-x^{m+1})\Big)
= x\max_{m\le n}|A_m|
\le \max_{m\le n}|A_m|.
]
So for (x\in[0,1]),
[
\sup_{x\in[0,1]}|P_n(x)|\le \max_{m\le n}|A_m|.
]

For (x\in[-1,0]), write (x=-y) with (y\in[0,1]) and set (b_k:=a_k(-1)^k
